title: Future Tense
situation: Make plans, promises, and predictions — talk about what's going to happen

phrases.english: I will do (male) / I will do (female)
phrases.context: Future: verb root + unga/ungi (for 'main'). Male = -unga, female = -ungi. Simple!

phrases.english: You will do (intimate-male) / You will do (intimate-female)
phrases.context: With 'tu': verb root + ega/egi. 'tu' is VERY informal — close-friends/playful only, sounds rude otherwise. Default to 'aap' or 'tum'. The ga/gi gender split applies throughout.

phrases.english: You will do (casual-male) / You will do (casual-female)
phrases.context: With 'tum': verb root + oge/ogi. The 'o' sound is the 'tum' marker in future

phrases.english: He will do / She will do
phrases.context: 'woh' uses same form as 'tu': -ega/-egi. Third person and intimate-you share the form

phrases.english: We will do (male group) / We will do (female group)
phrases.context: 'hum' = -enge/-engi. Also used for 'aap' (formal you) and 'woh' (they). The respectful/plural form

phrases.hindi: main kal aaunga / main kal aaungi
phrases.english: I'll come tomorrow (male) / I'll come tomorrow (female)
phrases.pronunciation: MAIN KAL aa-UN-ga / MAIN KAL aa-UN-gi

phrases.context: 'kab' = when. Just add question word — verb form doesn't change. Rising intonation optional

phrases.english: I'm coming right now (I'll be right there)
phrases.context: TRICK: Present tense used for immediate future! 'abhi aata hoon' = coming right now. Very common
'hogi' = will be/will happen (feminine, because 'baarish' is feminine). 'hona' → hoga/hogi/honge
Non-committal future! 'dekhte hain' = let's see. Indians use this to avoid saying no directly

grammar_notes: Future tense endings by person: main = -unga/-ungi, tu/woh = -ega/-egi, tum = -oge/-ogi, hum/aap/woh(plural) = -enge/-engi
Pattern: take verb root + the suffix. 'jaana'→root 'jaa': jaunga, jayega, jaoge, jayenge. 'khaana'→root 'kha': khaunga, khayega, khaoge, khayenge
The gender split is always -ga (male) vs -gi (female) at the end. This is the one consistent rule across all persons
'hona' (to be) in future: hoga (he/it-masc will be), hogi (she/it-fem will be), honge (they/we will be), houngi (I-fem will be)
Shortcut: Present tense + 'abhi/ab' often replaces future for immediate plans. 'Main abhi aata hoon' (I'm coming now) instead of 'main aaunga'
culture_notes: 'Dekhte hain' (let's see) is the polite Indian no. If someone says this about your plan, it probably won't happen
Indians frequently use 'kal' without specifying yesterday vs tomorrow — you're expected to figure it out. If confused, ask 'beeta kal ya aane wala kal?'

skill_breakdown.explanation: First person feminine: '-ungi' (main jaaungi). Masculine: '-unga'. Second/third: '-egi'/'-ega', plural: '-engi'/'-enge'. Pattern: stem + gender-marker + person-marker.
'-na hai' = soft obligation (I have to / I need to). '-na padega' = stronger obligation (I'll have to / will be forced to). Subject takes 'ko' marker: 'mujhe', 'aapko'.

more_examples.hindi: mujhe doctor ke paas jaana padega
more_examples.english: I'll have to go to the doctor

more_examples.hindi: ham sabko milkar kaam karna padega
more_examples.english: we'll all have to work together

skill_breakdown.explanation: 'pakka' (definitely / for sure) and 'zaroor' (certainly) are essential filler-words that make a future promise sound sincere. Drop them and you sound noncommittal.

practice_prompt: Practice future tense by making plans with me. Tell me what you'll do tomorrow, ask me when I'll arrive, or make predictions. I'll help you nail the -ga/-gi/-ge endings.
